pi_items.source: Task description explicitly states: 'save it to `/home/ga/Documents/LibreCAD/road_cross_section.dxf`'. Confirmed by setup_task.sh line 11 and export_result.sh line 7.
Task description 'Required Layers (Name - Color)' section lists exactly these 7 layers with their color assignments. Matches verifier.py line 64.
Task description states: 'Text Labels: "ASPHALT SURFACE", "AGGREGATE BASE", "SIDEWALK", "CURB & GUTTER", "CL" (at centerline)'. Metadata contains substring matches of these required labels.

Based on task requirements analysis: A complete road cross-section drawing must include pavement layers (minimum 2 lines), curbs (multiple lines), sidewalks (multiple lines), subgrade (1+ lines), dimensions (minimum 3), annotations (text labels, minimum 3), and centerline (1 dashed line). Total minimum of ~20+ entities is reasonable and standard for civil engineering CAD drawings.

AASHTO and state DOT standards specify minimum sidewalk width of 5 feet (4 feet minimum in constrained areas). Task specifies '5' wide' which matches standard practice. Task description states: 'Left Sidewalk: 5' wide (X=-21 to -16)'.

Standard practice for buffer strips between curb and sidewalk is 2-3 feet. Task specifies '2' wide grass strip' which falls within the acceptable range. Documented in Seattle Streets Illustrated and NJDOT design manuals.
Standard US lane width is 10-12 feet. Task specifies '12' wide' for each lane, which is the standard maximum and appropriate for residential streets. Task description: 'Left Lane: 12' wide'.

Vertical exaggeration ratios of 10:1 are standard practice in civil engineering cross-section drawings to make subtle slopes visible. Confirmed in ODOT, CDOT, and federal highway design manuals. Task description explicitly states: 'Vertical Exaggeration: 10:1 (1 ft vertical = 10 drawing units)'.
AASHTO and pavement design standards specify 4 inches minimum asphalt for low-traffic residential roads. Task specifies '4" Asphalt (draw 3.3 units thick)' which is the standard minimum thickness.

Pavement design standards specify 6-12 inches aggregate base for residential roads. Task specifies '8" Aggregate Base (draw 6.7 units thick)' which is within the standard range and appropriate for residential streets.

Task description geometry (X=-21 to X=21) yields 42 feet total. Breakdown: Left sidewalk (5') + buffer (2') + curb (2') + lane (12') + right lane (12') + curb (2') + buffer (2') + sidewalk (5') = 42 feet. This is reasonable for a two-lane residential street with sidewalks.
Task description states geometry is symmetric about X=0 and requires 'a vertical dashed line at X=0' for the CENTERLINE layer. This matches the drawing specification.

Inconsistency detected: Metadata required_text does NOT include 'CENTERLINE', but verifier.py line 94 includes 'CENTERLINE' in required_text_partials list. The verifier checks for 6 text items, but metadata only specifies 5. This is a discrepancy between metadata and implementation.

privileged_info_summary: The road cross-section task is a generative drawing task (not based on synthetic or real external data) requiring creation of a two-lane residential street profile in LibreCAD. All specifications are verified as consistent with civil engineering standards: 5-foot sidewalks (standard minimum), 2-foot buffers (standard practice), 12-foot lanes (standard width), 4-inch asphalt (minimum residential thickness), 8-inch aggregate base (standard), 10:1 vertical exaggeration (standard practice), and 42-foot total width. Seven required layers (PAVEMENT, CURB_GUTTER, SIDEWALK, SUBGRADE, DIMENSIONS, CENTERLINE, ANNOTATIONS) match the description. Required text labels (ASPHALT, AGGREGATE, SIDEWALK, CURB, CL) are appropriate for a cross-section drawing. Minimum 20 entities is reasonable for the specified geometry and annotations. One implementation inconsistency exists: the verifier checks for 'CENTERLINE' text (6 items), but metadata only requires 5 text items.
